old_claim.note: Blast Furnace provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The assay practice is commercially tied to mining valuation: it determines whether metal-bearing rock is ore worth mining and whether smelting will repay expense.

source_shape.source_locator: Introduction: the page says people define rock as ore by knowing metal content and recoverability, and that the assayer determines the amount of metal in the rock.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Fire assaying is presented as a way to determine the metal content of rock so mining decisions can distinguish ore from uneconomic material.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That application supports mining as the commercial context for the assay practice, while a blast furnace is a full-scale smelting technology outside this small-sample valuation scope.

ontology_before: Experimental Mining Assays implied that blast-furnace iron-production infrastructure directly enabled ore-assay valuation.
ontology_after: Mine Valuation Fire Assays depend on fire-assay workshop practice and mining valuation context, without a direct blast-furnace dependency.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: experimental_mining_assays now depends on mining and no longer directly depends on blast_furnace.
would_reject_if: A source showed that the scoped practice required full-scale blast furnaces rather than assay furnaces or small crucible/muffle furnace tests.
The node were broadened back to generic smelting experiments rather than mine-valuation fire assays.
A separate blast-furnace trial node were added and should preserve the blast_furnace edge.
short_reason: Mine-valuation assays are small-sample assay-furnace tests for mining decisions, not blast-furnace production.
